content:
# The Cartographer's Last Map

She found the map folded inside a library book about migratory birds — tucked between pages describing the Arctic tern, that restless creature that chases summer across the entire planet.

The paper was soft at the creases, worn to the texture of old skin. Someone had handled it many times, opening and refolding along the same lines until the fibers had nearly given up holding together. She spread it flat on the reading table and pressed her palms against it, feeling the slight ridges where a pen had moved with pressure and intention.

It wasn't a map of anywhere she recognized. The coastlines curved in directions that felt almost familiar — the way a word sounds almost right in a language you studied once and mostly forgot. Small notations crowded the margins in handwriting that leaned hard to the left, as though the writer had been bracing against something.

She looked for a name. Found only a date in the lower corner: March, no year given.

Outside the library window, a pigeon landed on the stone ledge and immediately left again, unsatisfied.

She thought about the hands that had made this. How they must have known exactly where they were going, or believed they did, which amounts to the same confidence if not the same destination.

She refolded the map carefully, returned it to the book, and slid the book back onto the shelf between the birds.
